name: Shepherds' Shelters Trail
name_original: Ruta de los Abrigos de los Pastores – circular desde Monterde de Albarracín (PR-TE 137)
description: Hike the moderate 8.9-mile Route of the Shepherds' Shelters (PR-TE 137) from Monterde de Albarracín, exploring forests and historic chozos.
region_intro: Hiking around Monterde De Albarracín offers exploration of the Iberian System mountains, characterized by varied terrain and significant natural features. The region is home to Europe's largest juniper forest, known as the sabinar, with ancient trees lining many paths. Geological formations of red sandstone, locally termed "rodeno," also define the landscape, providing unique scenery. The town itself is situated at an elevation of 1280 meters (about 4,200 feet) above sea level. Best hiking trails around Monterde De Albarracín The most popular hiking route is Sierra Alta – circular from Bronchales (PR-TE 131), a 5.5 miles (8.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 5 minutes to complete. This route offers panoramic views from Sierra Alta. Another top favourite among local hikers is Fuente del Canto Trail (PR-TE 130), a moderate 6.8 miles (10.9 km) path. This trail leads through diverse landscapes, including forested areas. Local hikers also love the Bezas - Guadalaviar Trail (PR-TE 1), a 13.8 miles (22.3 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 6 hours 25 minutes. Hiking around Monterde De Albarracín is defined by extensive juniper forests, red sandstone "rodeno" formations, and the mountainous terrain of the Iberian System. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult trails, catering to different ability levels. The routes in Monterde De Albarracín are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from around 180 reviews. More than 1000 hikers have used komoot to explore Monterde De Albarracín's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Monterde De Albarracín?
A: There are over 50 hiking trails around Monterde De Albarracín, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. The komoot community has explored these routes over 1000 times, with an average rating of 4.4 stars.

Q: What types of terrain and difficulty levels can I expect on the trails?
A: The trails in Monterde De Albarracín traverse the Iberian System mountains, featuring diverse terrain from extensive juniper forests to striking red sandstone formations known as "rodeno." You'll find a good mix of difficulties: 17 easy routes, 37 moderate routes, and 3 more challenging trails, catering to various fitness levels.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in the area?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes available. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979619171/sierra-alta-circular-from-bronchales-pr-te-131" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Alta – circular from Bronchales (PR-TE 131)&lt;/a&gt;, which offers panoramic views and is a popular choice among hikers.

Q: What natural attractions can I see while hiking in Monterde De Albarracín?
A: The region is home to Europe's largest juniper forest, the sabinar, with ancient trees lining many paths. You'll also encounter impressive red sandstone "rodeno" geological formations. For stunning vistas, consider visiting the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3620455" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Alta Summit&lt;/a&gt; or exploring the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4543105" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Municipal Park&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks near the hiking trails?
A: Yes, the red sandstone formations in the Municipal Park also house significant Levantine rock art, a UNESCO World Heritage site. Additionally, nearby attractions include the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/689101" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albarracín Old Town&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3978244" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Town of Albarracín&lt;/a&gt;, offering a cultural dimension to your outdoor excursions.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the trails?
A: The region boasts rich biodiversity. Hikers often spot deer, wild boars, hares, rabbits, squirrels, and foxes. The skies are frequented by griffon vultures, eagles, falcons, partridges, and wood pigeons, especially in the Sierra de Albarracín area.

Q: Are there any water features or rivers along the hiking routes?
A: Yes, the area includes picturesque water routes. One notable example is the Barranco de la Hoz, which follows the Río Blanco (White River) and features metallic walkways and a Toba bridge, offering unique scenery.

Q: What is the best time of year for hiking in Monterde De Albarracín?
A: The region offers a tranquil escape for nature lovers. While specific seasonal recommendations depend on your preference for weather, the diverse flora and fauna, including the deer rutting season, suggest that spring and autumn are particularly rewarding for wildlife spotting and comfortable temperatures. Summer can be warm, and winter may bring colder conditions at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any trails suitable for families with children?
A: Yes, some areas are well-suited for families. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3620455" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Alta Summit&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, is easily accessible and offers breathtaking panoramic views, making it suitable for families looking for less strenuous options.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Monterde De Albarracín?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from around 180 reviews. Hikers often praise the tranquility of the region, the unique experience of walking through Europe's largest juniper forest, and the stunning geological formations of red sandstone.

Q: Are there any guided hiking experiences available?
A: Yes, local initiatives like "Bronchales Experience" offer guided hiking and mountain biking (BTT) routes. These guided tours allow visitors to delve deeper into the region's flora and fauna and discover the secrets of the forests with expert insights.

Q: What is the longest moderate trail I can hike?
A: A longer moderate option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e981115873/shepherds-shelters-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Shepherds' Shelters Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which covers approximately 14.4 km (8.9 miles) and typically takes around 4 hours and 15 minutes to complete, offering a substantial journey through the landscape.

Q: Where can I find trails with significant elevation gain?
A: For trails with more significant elevation changes, consider routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974584093/bezas-guadalaviar-trail-pr-te-1" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bezas - Guadalaviar Trail (PR-TE 1)&lt;/a&gt;. This difficult 22.3 km (13.8 miles) trail involves an elevation gain of over 560 meters, providing a challenging experience for experienced hikers.
tips: This section of the Camino del Cid and coinciding with the GR 160 runs along a track in very good condition through the Alto de la Pedriza area that enters a very old and dense forest.

faqs.question: Where does the Route of the Shepherds' Shelters trail start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The circular route begins and ends in the tranquil village of Monterde de Albarracín. You should find parking available within or near the village, which serves as the main access point for the trail.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the PR-TE 137 trail?
faqs.answer: The trail offers diverse terrain, traversing deep pine forests, including parts of Europe's largest juniper forest. You'll encounter Jurassic limestones forming high plains (páramos) at 1,400-1,500 meters, broken up by ravines and sinkholes. Expect paths through scrubland, low mountain vegetation, some agricultural fields, and wooded areas dominated by holm oaks and junipers.

faqs.question: What unique historical features can I see along the Route of the Shepherds' Shelters?
faqs.answer: The trail's namesake, the traditional shepherds' shelters (chozos), are a highlight. These historic stone constructions, used for refuge, offer a glimpse into the region's pastoral life. You'll also pass the Campillo Cave, which has been restored to reflect its historical use, once accommodating up to 500 sheep.

faqs.question: Is the Route of the Shepherds' Shelters (PR-TE 137) suitable for beginners or families with children?
faqs.answer: This is classified as a 'moderate' hike, requiring a good level of fitness. While paths are mostly accessible, they do involve an elevation gain of about 420 meters over 14.4 kilometers. It might be challenging for absolute beginners or very young children, but active families with hiking experience could enjoy it.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the best times to hike this route. The weather is usually milder, and the natural scenery, from blooming wildflowers to autumn colors, is particularly beautiful. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring cold temperatures and snow at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the trail, given its location?
faqs.answer: The trail is located within the Paisaje Protegido de los Pinares de Rodeno. Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for hiking the PR-TE 137 itself. However, always respect local regulations for protected areas, such as staying on marked paths and not disturbing wildlife.

faqs.question: Is the Route of the Shepherds' Shelters dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While many trails in natural areas are dog-friendly, it's always advisable to keep your dog on a leash. This is especially important in areas with livestock (like sheep, given the trail's history) and to protect local wildlife, particularly within a protected landscape like the Paisaje Protegido de los Pinares de Rodeno.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with any other well-known hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other trails. You'll find sections that overlap with the Camino del Cid (both Senderista and BTT versions), the MontañasVacías route, and the Camino viejo de Bronchales a Albarracín.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife or plants might I encounter on the trail?
faqs.answer: The diverse landscape, including pine and juniper forests, scrubland, and rocky areas, supports varied flora and fauna. You might spot various butterfly species, and the region is known for its rich biodiversity. Keep an eye out for local birds and other small mammals typical of the Sierra de Albarracín.

faqs.question: Are there any viewpoints or particularly scenic spots along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers continuous natural beauty throughout its course. While specific named viewpoints aren't highlighted, the ascent towards the Campillo plains and the high páramos at 1,400-1,500 meters will provide expansive views over the diverse landscapes of the Sierra de Albarracín, including the vast juniper forests.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a moderate hike like the PR-TE 137?
faqs.answer: For a moderate hike of this duration and elevation, we recommend bringing plenty of water, snacks, appropriate hiking footwear, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing suitable for changing weather conditions. A map or GPS device is also advisable, even on a marked trail.

waypoints.name: Camino del Cid (Alto de la Pedriza)
